hypothesis: Code generation models will produce SQL injection vulnerabilities (CWE-89) when prompted to build database query functions without explicit security instructions.
method: Generated 50 Python functions that accept user input and query a SQLite database using 3 models (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Kimi K2.5). Each model received the same 50 prompts. Prompts varied in specificity: 20 generic ('write a function that queries users by name'), 15 moderately specific ('write a function that safely queries users'), 15 security-explicit ('write a parameterized query function'). Each generated function was tested against 12 SQL injection payloads including: basic tautology (1=1), UNION-based extraction, blind boolean, time-based blind, stacked queries, and comment-based bypasses. Detection method: static analysis (regex for string concatenation/f-string in SQL context) + dynamic testing (actual payload execution against test database).

payloads_most_effective: ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM admin --

conclusion: Prompt specificity is the strongest lever for secure code generation. Generic prompts produced vulnerable code 63% of the time vs 4.4% for security-explicit prompts — a 93% risk reduction. Model choice matters but less than prompt design: the gap between best and worst model (22% vs 52%) is smaller than the gap between generic and explicit prompts (63% vs 4.4%). For DST verification pipelines, the implication is clear: always include security context in generation prompts, and always run static analysis as a minimum gate. Two security-explicit prompts still produced vulnerable code, confirming that prompt engineering alone is insufficient — automated verification remains necessary.
notes: This is a baseline experiment. Follow-up experiments should test: (1) few-shot examples of secure code in the prompt, (2) chain-of-thought security reasoning, (3) automated repair of detected vulnerabilities, (4) expansion to other CWE classes. The two failures in security-explicit prompts both involved complex JOIN queries where the model used f-strings for table/column names (not just values) — a subtler pattern worth dedicated investigation.
